title: Narrative Reliefs From the SW and NW Corner Pavilions of Angkor Wat

This study examines the religious and social meaning of the reliefs from the western corner pavilions of the temple of Angkor Wat (12th century AD.). highlights: extensive bas-reliefs, and for the numerous devatas adorning its walls.  ...    king's state temple and capital city. As neither the foundation stela nor any contemporary inscriptions referring to the temple have been found, its original name is unknown, but it may have been known as "Varah Vishnu-lok" after the presiding deity. Work seems to have ended shortly after the king's death, leaving some of the bas-relief decoration unfinished. In 1177, approximately 27 years after the death of Suryavarman II, Angkor was sacked by the Chams, the traditional  ...    intended it to serve as his funerary temple.Further evidence for this view is provided by the bas-reliefs, which proceed in a counter-clockwise direction - prasavya in Hindu terminology - as this is the reverse of the normal order.  ...    its dedication to Vishnu, who was assoc  On the eastern gallery is one of the most celebrated scenes, the Churning of  ... title: [PDF] The hidden paintings of Angkor Wat
highlights:
bas-relief carvings that adorn the walls of  ...    around the temple and are divided into several scenes, including the famous bas-relief of the Churning of the Sea of Milk (East Gallery, South Wing) and Suryavarman II’s parade  ...    but is too degraded to identify positively (Figure 12). Angkor Wat is thought to be incomplete as the bas-reliefs along the north-east gallery enclosures were left unfinished and it is presumed that construction was abandoned after the death of Suryavarman II around 1150. This idea of abandonment is supported by the  discovery of drawings at the north-west corner of the second enclosure by Roveda (2000). C Antiquity Publications Ltd.
